title: Greece's Impossible Comeback
subtitle: The Battles of Salamis and Plataea
tagline: Thermopylae fell. Athens burned. The Persian Empire stood at the edge of everything. What came next was impossible.
villain_mandate: Xerxes must feel completely, genuinely unbeatable until the moment he isn't. His fleet at Salamis is the largest ever assembled. His army at Plataea outnumbers every Greek force combined. Every Greek victory must feel like it cost something enormous.

scenes.title: Athens Is Burning
scenes.narration: 480 BC. Athens is on fire. The Persian army has walked through the Spartan wall. The Acropolis is ash. Xerxes — King of Kings, ruler of the largest empire on Earth — sits on a golden throne above the sea and watches his fleet prepare to end Greece forever. He has twelve hundred warships. Greece has three hundred and seventy-one.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style, cinematic painting aesthetic. Athens burning on the horizon — the Acropolis in flames. Persian King Xerxes on a golden throne on a cliff overlooking the sea, watching his massive fleet below. Scale overwhelming — twelve hundred warships filling the water. The last Greek ships visible in the distance. 16:9 widescreen.

scenes.title: The Persian Trap
scenes.narration: Xerxes knew the Greek fleet would retreat to open water, where Persian numbers would crush them. Themistocles of Athens knew this too. So he did something that looked like surrender — he sent Xerxes a message: the Greek fleet was planning to flee during the night. Xerxes sealed the straits. The Greeks were trapped. This was exactly what Themistocles wanted.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Themistocles — weathered Athenian admiral, sharp calculating eyes — sending a secret messenger to the Persian fleet at night. Persian fleet moving to seal the Straits of Salamis, confident and massive. Map overlay showing the trap forming. The Persian commander reading the message, satisfied. He doesn't realize he is the one being manipulated. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Narrows
scenes.narration: Dawn. September 480 BC. The Persian fleet entered the Straits of Salamis — narrow waters where twelve hundred ships couldn't spread out. Where numbers became a liability. The Greek triremes — lighter, faster, more maneuverable — hit the Persian vanguard at ramming speed. The Persian fleet couldn't turn. Couldn't retreat. They were colliding with each other.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Dawn over the Straits of Salamis — narrow water, cliffs on both sides. Greek triremes ramming Persian ships at the point where the strait is tightest. Persian ships so crowded they cannot maneuver — colliding with their own fleet. The chaos of twelve hundred ships in a space built for a hundred. 16:9.

scenes.title: Xerxes Watches His Fleet Destroyed
scenes.narration: Xerxes watched from his throne on the hill as his invincible fleet sank. Ship after ship. Admiral after admiral. He had expected to watch a victory. He watched three hundred and seventy-one Greek ships destroy the greatest navy in the world. By nightfall, the Persian fleet was broken. But his army was still there. Four hundred thousand soldiers. Greece was not saved yet.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Xerxes on his golden throne on the cliff, expression moving from confident to disbelieving to cold rage as his ships sink below. Servants and generals averting their eyes. In the water: destroyed Persian ships, survivors in the sea. Greek triremes withdrawing in formation. The throne on the hill, suddenly very small. 16:9.

scenes.title: Xerxes Leaves — But Mardonius Stays
scenes.narration: Xerxes returned to Persia with his broken fleet, but he left behind his best general — Mardonius — with an army of three hundred thousand soldiers. The war was not over. Mardonius spent the winter. He burned Athens again. In 479 BC, he marched south to find the Greek alliance and finish what Xerxes had started. The Greeks had to stop him or there was nothing left.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Xerxes departing by sea, his fleet in ruins but his pride intact — he still has the largest land army on Earth behind him. Mardonius — experienced, ruthless, calculating — watching Xerxes leave, then turning back toward Greece. Athens shown burning for a second time. The scorched landscape of a war that will not end. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Greek Alliance
scenes.narration: For the first and only time, every major Greek city-state agreed to fight together. Sparta sent its full army — ten thousand Spartan warriors, the finest soldiers alive. Athens sent eight thousand. Corinth, Megara, every city that had anything left sent everything. One hundred and ten thousand Greeks against three hundred thousand Persians. The largest Greek army ever assembled still outnumbered three to one.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. The Greek allied army assembling on the plain before Plataea — Spartans in red cloaks at the front, Athenians beside them, coalition forces filling the ranks behind. The Persian camp visible in the distance — vastly larger. Military standards of every Greek city-state. A moment of unity that will never happen again. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Battle of Plataea
scenes.narration: 479 BC. Plataea. The Spartans held the right flank against the elite Persian infantry — the Immortals. For hours they held. Mardonius himself led the charge against the Spartan line. He died there. When the Persian general fell, his army broke. What three hundred thousand soldiers couldn't do in months, one Spartan spear did in one moment.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Battle of Plataea — Spartans in red cloaks holding the right flank against the Persian Immortals in full armor. Brutal close combat. Mardonius on horseback leading the charge, then falling. The moment his standard drops. The Persian formation fracturing — first a crack, then a rout. Spartans advancing. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Persian Empire Retreats
scenes.narration: The Persian retreat became a rout. Three hundred thousand soldiers fleeing across a battlefield they had entered with total confidence. The Greek alliance pursued. The Persian camp — the richest camp anyone had ever seen, full of Xerxes's gold and silk tents and treasure — fell to the Greeks. The richest empire on Earth had been turned back by city-states that couldn't agree on anything.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Persian retreat in chaos — soldiers abandoning their camp, the Greek pursuit pressing them. The Persian camp falling: silk tents, gold vessels, treasures of the empire strewn across the battlefield. Greek soldiers stopping in disbelief at the wealth around them. The scale of what was left behind tells you the scale of what was lost. 16:9.

scenes.title: What Greece Kept
scenes.narration: The Persian Wars were over. Greece had survived the largest empire in human history — twice. Salamis and Plataea didn't just save Greece. They saved everything that came after: Socrates, Plato, Aristotle. Democracy. The entire foundation of Western civilization. None of it exists if Xerxes wins at Salamis. Themistocles's trick in the dark saved the future.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Athens beginning to rebuild — workers on the Acropolis, the Parthenon rising. Philosophers in the agora. Ships in the rebuilt harbor. The city that was burned twice is becoming the city that will define civilization. Wide panoramic shot, golden afternoon. The cost of what was saved is visible in every damaged stone. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Impossible Comeback
scenes.narration: The largest fleet ever built. The largest army ever assembled. The richest empire in the world. All of it turned back by three hundred and seventy-one ships and a trick sent in the dark. History remembers Thermopylae — the three hundred who held the pass. But Salamis and Plataea are where Greece actually survived. Subscribe to Gods & Glory for the battles that changed everything.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Final card — the Straits of Salamis at dawn, calm water where the greatest naval battle in the ancient world was fought. Then: the plains of Plataea, quiet. Then: the Acropolis, rebuilt, gleaming. Three images of what was preserved. The cost was everything. The result was civilization. 16:9.

viral_hook: Athens was burning. Xerxes had twelve hundred warships. Greece had three hundred and seventy-one. What happened next was impossible.
highlight_note: Scene 4 — Xerxes watching his fleet destroyed from his throne. The expression change. The golden throne suddenly very small.
youtube_title: Greece's Impossible Comeback | Salamis & Plataea | Gods & Glory
lesson: The largest force doesn't always win. The right terrain, the right trick, and the willingness to fight on borrowed time can stop an empire.
